title: How to Sketch a Learning Algorithm
speaker: Sam Gunn
abstract:
How does the choice of training data influence an AI model? This question is of central importance to interpretability, privacy, and basic science.  At its core is the data deletion problem: after a reasonable amount of precomputation, quickly predict how the model would behave if a given subset of training data had been deleted.
I will present a data deletion scheme capable of predicting model outputs with arbitrarily small error ε and failure probability δ in the deep learning setting.  The precomputation and prediction algorithms are only Õ(log(1/δ)/ε^2) factors slower than regular training and inference, respectively. The storage requirements are those of Õ(log(1/δ)/ε^2) models. 
The proof is based on a simple assumption about the stability of training. In contrast to the assumptions made by prior work, stability appears to be fully compatible with learning powerful AI models.  At a technical level, our work is based on a new method for locally sketching an arithmetic circuit by computing higher-order derivatives in random complex directions.
Based on my recent work: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.07328.

proof_stages.label: Retraining bottleneck behind deletion, attribution, and selection
proof_stages.summary: The talk identifies a common obstacle in AI workflows: changing training data, unlike changing an inference query, normally requires slow retraining, which underlies machine unlearning, data attribution, data selection, poisoning defense, and influence-function questions.

proof_stages.label: Data deletion scheme and target guarantee
proof_stages.summary: The desired object is defined as a slow precomputation phase that may store auxiliary information and a fast prediction phase that, after seeing the data to delete, approximates the outcome of actual retraining with error epsilon and roughly a one-over-epsilon-squared overhead.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to preprocessing an arithmetic-circuit data model
proof_stages.summary: After fixing the dataset, randomness, scalar test function, and deletion weights, the learning-and-testing process is modeled as an arithmetic circuit f from deletion vectors to outcomes, so the mathematical task becomes compressing f for fast evaluation on indicator vectors.

proof_stages.label: Stability as exponential Taylor-coefficient decay
proof_stages.summary: The key assumption is that the norms of the multivariate Taylor tensors of f decay exponentially with order, an assumption motivated by derivative-norm experiments, interpreted as low-complexity higher-order data interactions, and partially testable by estimating low-order tensor norms.

proof_stages.label: Taylor truncation and the infeasible coefficient-table scheme
proof_stages.summary: Stability suggests truncating the Taylor expansion at logarithmic order in one over epsilon, but the naive precomputation that stores all Taylor tensors is impossible because an order-r tensor has size n to the r.

proof_stages.label: Random-projection sketches of Taylor contractions
proof_stages.summary: The tensor contraction needed for evaluation is replaced by an unbiased random-projection estimator whose variance is dimension-independent under stability, and then by a symmetric complex rank-one projection so that the random vector itself has only length n.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial-ring evaluation computes the sketches implicitly
proof_stages.summary: To avoid materializing Taylor tensors, f is evaluated on z times the random projection vector over a truncated complex polynomial ring, whose coefficients are exactly the desired projected Taylor terms, yielding the final repeated sketching precomputation and fast prediction algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Removing the fixed-query simplification
proof_stages.summary: The scheme is extended from a hard-coded test function to later-chosen queries by running only the slow training part over the polynomial ring during precomputation, storing polynomial-valued model parameters, and letting prediction finish the fast inference computation.

examples.name: Jackson Pollock unlearning request
examples.note: A stylized artist asks that all of his data be removed from training, illustrating why fast prediction of the retrained-without-that-data model is needed; the later audience question clarifies that the deletion scheme should match the retraining outcome, not preserve the original output.
examples.engagement_reason: the Pollock deletion request is used as a motivating scenario for unlearning, with no concrete computation or algorithm run on Pollock’s data

examples.name: Jackson Pollock data-attribution probability drop
examples.note: The speaker imagines comparing the probability of a Pollock-like output before and after deleting Pollock's training data, illustrating data attribution as another instance of the retraining bottleneck.
examples.engagement_reason: the probability drop after deleting Pollock’s data is described hypothetically as a data-attribution test, but no probability is actually computed

examples.name: Neural-network Taylor-coefficient decay experiments
examples.note: Derivative-norm plots for ResNet, with a brief comparison to micro-GPT experiments, give empirical evidence that exponential Taylor-coefficient decay can occur in real learning systems, motivating but not proving the stability assumption.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker discusses actual Taylor-derivative norm experiments, interprets the decay plot, runs, orders, and relation to the stability assumption
